sentences.text: My name is Emory Angles.
I was born in Budapest on the 21st of October, 1924.
My father's name was Lajos Engel, E-N-G-E-L.
It was spelled differently.
My mother's name was Gisella Engel.
Well, I-- we lived in Ujpest, which is a suburb of Budapest, about 6 kilometers from Budapest.
It's a township which had about 100,000 inhabitants.
We lived in number one Kiraly street.
Kiraly means in Hungarian King.
So I lived in number one King Street.
It was a flat house, as we called them at the time.
And we had a flat on the first floor.
It was a typical dwelling for middle class citizens of-- of the time.
My early childhood was completely absorbed in the times, which-- which meant that we lived on the strong discipline in our school.
We have obeyed our teachers, our professors to the letter.
And we worked really hard.
The school I started in had over 150 people starting-- pupils starting.
And by the time we finished, we had, I think, 28 or so matriculating, which meant that you had to be really talented, or work as hard as I did.
I said, or work as hard as I did.
So I gave you the answer.
No, it was a public school.
It was a state school.
We didn't have private schools of the types we have here today.
And not speaking of the fact that we could not afford the expenses attached to it.
It was a public school.
It was a-- a state supported funded school.
And as it turned out to be, as I compared my early education with what I've seen all over the world, it was an excellent school.
At the time I wasn't fully aware of that, because I thought that, you know, being in Hungary, part of the Balkan or very close to it, we have an inferior education.
Far from it.
I think the curriculum, what we were taught, was excellent.
But most importantly, we have learned discipline, and we learned how to behave for the rest of our lives.
And that was very valuable.

Yes, discipline was ruling our home life as well, because obviously what we were taught in the school was in the atmosphere, in the atmosphere that was carried over in all aspects of life, including home life.
However, my home life was essentially-- I-- I would not say that my home life was ruled by discipline.
It was a mixture of discipline and-- and-- and family love, which was an excellent mixture.
And-- and-- you know, we-- in our family, we all knew where we stood, we know what-- what our individual rules and obligations were.
My obligation was to-- to get through my studies and make sure that I don't fail.
And-- and my father was in a very modest financial circumstances.
We had to pay a rather high tuition fee to the school.
And it would have been an issue of letting him down if I was-- had I been unable to cope with the issues.
I was the only child.
Only my father, my mother, and myself.

Very strangely, while our grandparents' family was always [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] marking a large family of seven, eight, nine children, the next generation, my mother's, my father's generation, usually had only one, or maximum, two children.
Look, I-- interesting question.
I think the reason my grandparents had so many children was, I imagine, a religious issue primarily.

Beyond that, in those days-- although I must say, perhaps what I was going to say is not quite correct, because my motherly grandfather was a baker in a small provincial town in-- in Hungary who had eight children, managed to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] achieve professional status for his sons.
It was one doctor and two lawyers amongst them.
Very strangely, one of the girls was a lawyer, which was an unbelievably avant garde activity at those time, particularly for a Jewish girl.
One son stayed a baker, and he was the black sheep of the family.
And the other girls, including my mother, they got married.
That was their-- their-- their-- their fate, and their destiny-- their destination.
I'll give you a good example what the expectation was in my grandfather's time, very typical story.
As I said, there was eight children.
The children could not get the type of attention what a child gets in a single child family, like I did.
So they had eight children.
And one of the boys-- whose name was Emery also, I was named after him, who became a doctor.
At the age of 14, he was a strong-willed young child.
He decided that he had enough of this education.
He wants to finish, right?
So my grandmother, who was the-- who was wearing the trousers, I believe, in the family, said to him, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] fine.
Emery uses a very old legend in the family.
You see, they lived in the-- they lived in the Slovak part of Hungary, in the upper part.
And he came back and he said in Slovak, which is-- I don't understand Slovak, but the word stuck with me.
[SLOVAK].
It means, I believe, that I have learned enough.
I know everything now, right?
So my grandmother said, [SLOVAK]?
OK, I mean, if you know everything, here's your rucksack.
Go out and earn your living, and prove it to me, right?
So Emery was put out in the middle of the night.
And he went out.
He lasted a few hours.
He knocked on the door.
He came back and he said, I suppose I-- I-- I ought to continue studying.
So that was the mentality at the time regarding these bourgeois middle class, lower middle class families, who were-- where the parents made a supreme effort to educate their children.
And what was your mother like?
My mother--
Yes, I-- I do remember elementary school quite well, as a matter of fact.
I went to a school, which was also a state school near to where we lived.
It was in the Alzbet Street, Elizabeth Street.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was-- it was, again, a fairly, you know, fairly competitive [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] school on its own level, of course.
I remember very clearly, and that is again rather-- rather-- rather characteristic to the times, of course we were living in the early-- after the first war-- irredentistic times.
And at this early stage, they have instilled into us the injustice which happened to Hungary after being-- it has been dismembered after the First World War.
And on one occasion we went to see-- the school took out-- took us out to see [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] such a play.
And after the play we were assigned the duty of writing about the play, the way we felt about the play.
And I, of course, like, was a particularly impressionable young boy.

And I wrote-- I wrote a summary of the play, and I finished up somewhat by saying that, yes, and I would like to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] sink my sword into the heart of that awful-- I think it was a Czech person displayed in this-- in this play.
And I won the award, right, because I have-- I have-- you know, it was such a-- such a good summary of the play.
Which was, again, very characteristic, you know, in those times.
Unfortunately, looking back to it, we had been taught excessive nationalism, which is no good in its excessiveness.

And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] those children-- and I was one of them-- who were good material to respond for that sort of exposure, had been given awards, winning the first prize in the-- in the summary.
And all in all, that-- that feeling, that overreaction to that official doctrine has been the cornerstone of a young Hungarian boy's-- Jewish or otherwise-- education.
This is very interesting.
Well, of course I have absorbed the religious part of the Jewish component from my grandparents, my motherly grandparents.
So when we went for Pesach, when we went for religious occasions, as I said to you earlier, we lived in the detached part of Hungary, which belonged to Czechoslovakia at the time.
We had to-- had to get a passport every time to go there.
So it was quite a big journey.
Well, we would have-- my mother was very, very strongly attached to her family.
And we would have gone at least [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] once, twice a year.
But I would like to emphasize to you that although [NON-ENGLISH], where we lived, was only, oh, about-- oh, about, I imagine it wouldn't be more than-- 150, 180 kilometers from Budapest.
That was a major journey.
I mean, we had to cross borders.
We had to have a passport.
And it was a financially, also, demanding exercise.
But we went there because that was the family-- family tradition.
And occasionally my father came, too.
Most of the time my father came, too.
My father's parents lived in Nitra, which is also in the same part of Hungary, about 30, 40 kilometers north from [NON-ENGLISH] we were.
But to answer your question, which was what was my early Jewish influence in my life, so that's where I absorbed the ritual part of it.
My grandmother, I would like to describe her to you.
Very interesting personality.
My grandfather died in '29, so he wasn't around by that time.
And my grandmother-- grandmother became the matriarch of the family.
Strong-willed, educated person, which was quite unusual at those time.
He deeply believed in God.
And when my grandfather died, she simply put is as the will of the almighty, right?
And she carried on with her life with the support of her children, who are-- who had been living in the area there.
She was kosher.
She was-- she was strictly kosher.
She was praying regularly, every-- every-- every day.
And he kept Shabbat, also.
So the-- the ritual part of the religious education I absorbed there.
My grandmother was a tremendous family person.
And in conformity with the philosophy of those times, being Jewish, being religious, Jewish, completely occupied her-- her-- her emotional life.
By which I mean that she had no-- she had a big heart.
She gave everything for the family.
However, with retrospect, I realize that she was-- because of-- of the fact that the family occupied all-- all of her heart, she was unable to give-- nothing was left for anybody else.
So certainly, nothing was left-- was nothing left for-- for the neighbor, who happened to be non-Jewish, of course, which wasn't in his favor in any case, in my grandmother's philosophy.
And-- and she didn't have a great deal of time for even her Jewish neighbor, had it been a Jewish neighbor.
Because the family was all her life.
And that took all her energy, all her emotional reserves.
So that was my-- that was my Jewish background coming from there as a small child.
I was the only boy in the family as a grandchild.
All the others were girls, so that secured a very strong position for me on the top of that, you see?
And-- and-- and that background was passed onto my mother, who had a kosher household.
Not-- not as strict as my grandmother, but it was kosher.
I remember we-- we had the-- had been taking the chickens to be killed by the religious ritual, et cetera.
And we went to shul, we went-- we observed the major-- major-- major Jewish holidays.
However, our religious activity was-- was somewhat diluted in comparison to what I've seen at my grandfathers, grandmothers place.
Now, this religious background, of course, was mixed, as I originally told you, with the Hungarian [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] education system, right?
And the-- the-- my peers, my school surrounding, were so strong that my younger childhood was completely taken over by it.

And while I was Jewish, I went to Jewish synagogues, and I-- I didn't go to school on main Jewish holidays, I was a person who was reciting [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] nationalistic poems during the school holidays in the school because I was good at that.
And my parents came along.
And they were very, very happy to see me performing in front of 600 children and the-- and the professors.
And I was very proud of it, and I was very pleased that I could please them.
That was my background.
And what is interesting that when the change started to come, and that started to come from when the first anti-Jewish laws came into being.
Then-- then gradually, this-- this Jewish-- this-- this Hungarian background, rather, was slowly pushed into the background.
And I was starting to-- to search myself, to query the situation.
I looked into the mirror and I thought to myself, gee, I must be a pretty awful person if what I can hear from the official papers and-- and all other medias is true.
Well, look, the-- the first Jewish-- anti-Jewish law, it was a very systematic, constitutional issue.
It has been-- has been carried by the Hungarian parliament by a majority, a very sizable majority.
And of course, you must understand the political global-- I mean, European global background to it.

And in return for that, Hungary obtained certain preferential treatment, by which I mean that the detached parts of Hungary, which were a sore point, as I said earlier, in a Hungarian historical mind, were reattached back to Hungary.
And because of that, of course, Hungary was quasi-rewarded, right, for towing the German line.
Oh, absolutely.
I was delirious when-- when the northern part of Hungary returned.
Because my-- in my mind, I thought, well next time we go to [NON-ENGLISH], we don't have to go by passport.
And those-- those ugly Czechs, you know, whom as a child of eight, I would have killed with a sword, right?
Will not to rule that part of historical Hungary.
What I thought of that, well, it was very interesting.
It was a very interesting-- interesting mixture, as I said to you earlier.
I started to-- I started to query things.
And-- and it took me a year, a couple of years, indeed, to realize that this is nothing but ugly propaganda, right?
I'm not as ugly as they paint me.
And-- but during those two years, I remember I was anxious to recite those poems even better.
I was anxious to kick another goal for our team, just to prove that, you know, I can do better than you can, right?
And-- and as I said, it was a-- it was a-- a transitional time of two years before I realized that I'm right and you are wrong.
I was-- I was 13, 14, 15.
That's-- that's-- that was the time.
From my parents?
Look, my parents, my father was essentially a conventional, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] meek, law-abiding citizen.
His-- his behavior, and therefore his example to me-- to me was that, look, this is the law.
We have to abide the law, right?
And that was the very interesting part of the whole anti-Jewish campaign, which was getting uglier and uglier.
Even the last, most ugly scene, when-- when [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the crease appeared on the wall that you have to go to the gas chamber, right?
I mean, I'm speaking figuratively, but very close to the truth, because there's no gas chambers in Hungary.
The general attitude of my father, or the type of Jew he was, is that let's move.
We have to go.
Pack up and let's go.
Let's do what they tell us we must do, right?
So that's a sort of support, because that was your question, that was the mentality behind the support I was getting from my parents.
My mother was a-- a gentlewoman.
She had no-- no political stand, belief, right?
She cared for me, and that's where it all ended.
And aside from that systematic and bureaucratic signaling of anti-Semitism, did you see evidence of overt anti-Semitism at school amongst people you knew?
Oh, absolutely.
Very early in-- in-- in my elementary school, I remember it was a young bully there, you know?
And-- and it was, you know, on a popular level, on the-- on the-- on the level what became [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] became almost a slogan, right?

When he told me during the break in the schoolyard, something to the effect that you're a dirty Jew, I'm not quite sure what has provoked it and what were the circumstances, but I remember it very clearly.
And I would have been eight or nine at the time.
So I just went there, and I-- I smacked him on the nose.
And his nose bleeded.
And it was the last-- the last time that he ever said that.
That was the-- that was the-- the early experience I had in-- in-- in these matters.
Well, there was numerous clauses which excluded Jewish students to go to university.
Incidentally, these numerous clauses, for historical clarity, I would like to say, was enforced way back.
I believe it came into being after the-- after 1919 or 1920, as early as that.
Or certainly in the early '20s.
And the paradoxical situation arose as a result of it, that because in Hungary, even well before the war-- I was born in '24.
If a Jewish intellectual wanted to pursue tertiary education, sometimes he had to go to Berlin, right?
Before Hitler, right?
Because in Hungary, the numerous clauses restricted the Jewish participation to I think 6%.
Did you want to go to university?
Of course I wanted to go to university.
My-- you asked me about my-- my-- my high school days, right?
And anti-Semitism during that, is that correct?
Well, now we are entering into-- into-- I matriculated in 1942, which was already an advanced stage of the war, and certainly an advanced stage of the German influence in Hungary.
The local population's reactions should be, perhaps, explained, right?
Anti-Semitism in Hungary was a fairly inherent issue in the population.
Hungary was an unfortunate country, unfortunate from the point of view that it always managed to attached itself to the loser.
In the First World War, they managed to lose.
The Second World War, eventually they lost.
And when you are-- that-- that had a stronger influence on the Hungarian historical times in the '20s and '30s.
And when a country is-- is-- is defeated, you're always looking for-- for scapegoats.
And the Jews became the scapegoats.
They were becoming the reason, plausible reason why a lot of the setbacks have occurred.
And it-- like any minority in a-- in a-- in a-- in a country, times were not as liberal as here today in Australia, where anything and everybody goes.
